([/eɪmz/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is a city in [Story County, Iowa](/wiki/Story_County,_Iowa "Story County, Iowa"), United States, located approximately 30 miles (48 km) north of [Des Moines](/wiki/Des_Moines,_Iowa "Des Moines, Iowa") in central Iowa. It is the home of [Iowa State University](/wiki/Iowa_State_University "Iowa State University") (ISU). According to the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Ames had a population of 66,427, making it the state's [ninth-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Iowa "List of cities in Iowa").[[5]](#cite_note-cen2020-5) Iowa State University was home to 30,177 students as of fall 2023,[[6]](#cite_note-enrollment-6) which make up approximately one half of the city's population.

**Ames** ([/eɪmz/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is a city in [Story County, Iowa](/wiki/Story_County,_Iowa "Story County, Iowa"), United States, located approximately 30 miles (48 km) north of [Des Moines](/wiki/Des_Moines,_Iowa "Des Moines, Iowa") in central Iowa. It is the home of [Iowa State University](/wiki/Iowa_State_University "Iowa State University") (ISU). According to the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Ames had a population of 66,427, making it the state's [ninth-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Iowa "List of cities in Iowa").[[5]](#cite_note-cen2020-5) Iowa State University was home to 30,177 students as of fall 2023,[[6]](#cite_note-enrollment-6) which make up approximately one half of the city's population.

A [United States Department of Energy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy "United States Department of Energy") national laboratory, [Ames Laboratory](/wiki/Ames_Laboratory "Ames Laboratory"), is located on the ISU campus. Ames also hosts [United States Department of Agriculture](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Agriculture "United States Department of Agriculture") (USDA) sites: the largest federal animal disease center in the United States, the USDA Agricultural Research Service's National Animal Disease Center (NADC),[[7]](#cite_note-7) as well as one of two national USDA sites for the [Animal and Plant Health Inspection Service](/wiki/Animal_and_Plant_Health_Inspection_Service "Animal and Plant Health Inspection Service") (APHIS), which comprises the [National Veterinary Services Laboratory](/wiki/National_Veterinary_Services_Laboratory "National Veterinary Services Laboratory") and the Center for Veterinary Biologics.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Ames also hosts the headquarters of the [Iowa Department of Transportation](/wiki/Iowa_Department_of_Transportation "Iowa Department of Transportation").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ames,_Iowa&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The city was founded in 1864 as a station stop on the [Cedar Rapids and Missouri Railroad](/wiki/Cedar_Rapids_and_Missouri_Railroad "Cedar Rapids and Missouri Railroad") and was named after 19th century U.S. Congressman [Oakes Ames](/wiki/Oakes_Ames "Oakes Ames") of Massachusetts, who was influential in the building of the [transcontinental railroad](/wiki/First_transcontinental_railroad_(North_America) "First transcontinental railroad (North America)").[[9]](#cite_note-9) Ames was founded by local resident Cynthia Olive Duff (née Kellogg) and railroad magnate [John Insley Blair](/wiki/John_Insley_Blair "John Insley Blair"),[[10]](#cite_note-10) near a location that was deemed favorable for a railroad crossing of the [Skunk River](/wiki/Skunk_River_(Iowa) "Skunk River (Iowa)") and [Ioway Creek](/wiki/Ioway_Creek_(Ames,_Iowa) "Ioway Creek (Ames, Iowa)"). William West (1821–1919) became the first mayor of Ames in 1870. With his wife Harriet, from 1869 to 1892, he ran Ames's first hotel, known as West House, on Douglas Avenue on the site of the present Octagon Center for the Arts. West was a highly respected pioneer businessman who also served on the Ames School Board in the 1880s when Central School was built on the site of the present Ames City Hall on Clark Avenue and Sixth Street. The Wests raised several daughters and sons. William West spent the last of his life living with his son in northwest Iowa.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ames,_Iowa&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

Ames is located along the western edge of [Story County](/wiki/Story_County,_Iowa "Story County, Iowa"), roughly 30 miles (48 km) north of the state capital, [Des Moines](/wiki/Des_Moines,_Iowa "Des Moines, Iowa"). Passing through Ames is the cross country line of the [Union Pacific Railroad](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Railroad "Union Pacific Railroad") and two small streams (the South Skunk River and [Ioway Creek](/wiki/Ioway_Creek_(Ames,_Iowa) "Ioway Creek (Ames, Iowa)")).

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 24.27 square miles (62.86 km2), of which 24.21 square miles (62.70 km2) is land and 0.06 square miles (0.16 km2) is water.[[12]](#cite_note-Gazetteer_files-12)

* [Ames Main Street Historic District](/wiki/Ames_Main_Street_Historic_District "Ames Main Street Historic District")
* Campustown, south of Iowa State University, is a high-density mixed-use neighborhood with many student apartments, nightlife venues, and restaurants.[[13]](#cite_note-AmesPlan-13)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ames,_Iowa&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate")]

Ames has a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Dfa*). On average, the warmest month is July and the coldest is January. The highest recorded temperature was 109 °F (43 °C) on July 24, 1901, and the lowest was −37 °F (−38 °C) January 25, 1894.[[14]](#cite_note-XMACIS2-14)

### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ames,_Iowa&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

As of the [census](/wiki/United_States_census "United States census") of 2020,[[17]](#cite_note-17) there were 66,427 people, 25,579 households, and 10,641 families residing in the city. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 2,407.5 inhabitants per square mile (929.5/km2). There were 27,806 housing units at an average density of 1,007.8 per square mile (389.1/km2). The [racial](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States_census "Race and ethnicity in the United States census") makeup of the city was 77.5% [White](/wiki/White_Americans "White Americans"), 4.2% [Black or African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), 0.3% [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), 7.9% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans"), 0.0% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_Americans "Pacific Islander Americans"), 3.1% from other races and 6.9% from two or more races. [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") persons of any race comprised 5.9% of the population.

Of the 25,579 households, 17.5% of which had children under the age of 18 living with them, 32.5% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 6.8% were cohabiting couples, 29.5% had a female householder with no spouse or partner present and 31.2% had a male householder with no spouse or partner present. 58.4% of all households were non-families. 35.9% of all households were made up of individuals, 8.1% had someone living alone who was 65 years old or older.

The median age in the city was 23.6 years. 26.3% of the residents were under the age of 20; 27.7% were between the ages of 20 and 24; 22.4% were from 25 and 44; 12.8% were from 45 and 64; and 10.8% were 65 years of age or older. The gender makeup of the city was 53.0% male and 47.0% female.

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ames,_Iowa&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

As of the census[[18]](#cite_note-wwwcensusgov-18) of 2010, there were 58,965 people, 22,759 households, and 9,959 families residing in the city. The population density was 2,435.6 inhabitants per square mile (940.4/km2). There were 23,876 housing units at an average density of 986.2 per square mile (380.8/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 84.5% White, 3.4% African American, 0.2% Native American, 8.8% Asian, 1.1% from other races, and 2.0% from two or more races. Hispanic or Latino of any race were 3.4% of the population.

There were 22,759 households, of which 19.1% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 35.6% were married couples living together, 5.4% had a female householder with no husband present, 2.7% had a male householder with no wife present, and 56.2% were non-families. 30.5% of all households were made up of individuals, and 6.2% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.25 and the average family size was 2.82.

The median age in the city was 23.8 years. 13.4% of residents were under the age of 18; 40.5% were between the ages of 18 and 24; 22.9% were from 25 to 44; 15% were from 45 to 64; and 8.1% were 65 years of age or older. The gender makeup of the city was 53.0% male and 47.0% female.

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ames,_Iowa&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

As of the census of 2000,[[19]](#cite_note-GR2-19) there were 50,731 people, 18,085 households, and 8,970 families residing in the city. The population density was 2,352.3 inhabitants per square mile (908.2/km2). There were 18,757 housing units at an average density of 869.7 per square mile (335.8/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 87.34% White, 7.70% Asian, 2.65% African American, 0.04% Native American, 0.76% Pacific Islander and other races, and 1.36% from two or more races. Hispanic or Latino of any race were 1.98% of the population.

There were 18,085 households, out of which 22.3% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 42.0% were married couples living together, 5.3% had a female householder with no husband present, and 50.4% were non-families. 28.5% of all households were made up of individuals, and 5.9% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.30 and the average family size was 2.85.

Age spread: 14.6% under the age of 18, 40.0% from 18 to 24, 23.7% from 25 to 44, 13.9% from 45 to 64, and 7.7% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 24 years. For every 100 females, there were 109.3 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 109.9 males.

The median income for a household in the city was $36,042, and the median income for a family was $56,439. Males had a median income of $37,877 versus $28,198 for females. The per capita income for the city was $18,881. About 7.6% of families and 20.4% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line_in_the_United_States "Poverty line in the United States"), including 9.2% of those under age 18 and 4.1% of those age 65 or over.

The [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/U.S._Census_Bureau "U.S. Census Bureau") designates the Ames [MSA](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") as encompassing all of Story County. While Ames is the largest city in Story County, the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") is in the nearby city of [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada,_Iowa "Nevada, Iowa"), 8 miles (13 km) east of Ames.

Ames metropolitan statistical area combined with the [Boone, Iowa](/wiki/Boone,_Iowa "Boone, Iowa") [micropolitan statistical area](/wiki/Micropolitan_statistical_area "Micropolitan statistical area") ([Boone County, Iowa](/wiki/Boone_County,_Iowa "Boone County, Iowa")) make up the larger Ames-Boone [combined statistical area](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area"). Ames is the larger principal city of the Combined Statistical Area that includes all of Story County, Iowa and Boone County, Iowa.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) which had a combined population of 106,205 at the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census").[[19]](#cite_note-GR2-19)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ames,_Iowa&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Economy")]

Iowa State University contains the [U.S. Department of Agriculture](/wiki/U.S._Department_of_Agriculture "U.S. Department of Agriculture")'s National Animal Disease Center, which developed the [Ames strain](/wiki/Ames_strain "Ames strain")), and the [U.S. Department of Energy](/wiki/U.S._Department_of_Energy "U.S. Department of Energy")'s [Ames Laboratory](/wiki/Ames_Laboratory "Ames Laboratory"), a major materials research and development facility.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Located in Ames are the main offices of the [Iowa Department of Transportation](/wiki/Iowa_Department_of_Transportation "Iowa Department of Transportation"), and state and Federal institutions are the largest employers in Ames.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The [Iowa State University Research Park](/wiki/ISU_Research_Park "ISU Research Park") is a [not-for-profit](/wiki/Not-for-profit "Not-for-profit") business development incubator located in Ames, and affiliated with Iowa State University.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The Bureau of Labor Statistics ranked Ames and [Boulder, Colorado](/wiki/Boulder,_Colorado "Boulder, Colorado") as having the lowest unemployment rate (2.5%) of any metropolitan area in the United States in 2016.[[25]](#cite_note-25) By June 2018, unemployment in Ames had fallen even further, to 1.5%, though wage increases for workers were not keeping pace with rising rents.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ames,_Iowa&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

* The Ames History Museum was founded in 1980, and includes a historic schoolhouse.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* Ames Public Library, a [Carnegie library](/wiki/Carnegie_library "Carnegie library"),[[29]](#cite_note-carnegieiowa-29) was founded in 1904.[[30]](#cite_note-aplhistory-30)

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ames,_Iowa&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Sports")]

The [Iowa State Cyclones](/wiki/Iowa_State_Cyclones "Iowa State Cyclones") play a variety of sports in the Ames area. The [Iowa State Cyclones football](/wiki/Iowa_State_Cyclones_football "Iowa State Cyclones football") team plays at [Jack Trice Stadium](/wiki/Jack_Trice_Stadium "Jack Trice Stadium") in Ames.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The Cyclones' Men's and Women's Basketball teams and Volleyball teams play at [Hilton Coliseum](/wiki/Hilton_Coliseum "Hilton Coliseum").[[33]](#cite_note-33) The Iowa State Cyclones are a charter member of the [Big 12 Conference](/wiki/Big_12_Conference "Big 12 Conference") in all sports and compete in [NCAA](/wiki/NCAA "NCAA") Division I-A.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Parks and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ames,_Iowa&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Parks and recreation")]

Ames has multiple parks, including Brookside Park, River Valley Park and the Ada Hayden Heritage Park, which contains a lake, a series of wetlands and trails.[[35]](#cite_note-35) [Reiman Gardens](/wiki/Reiman_Gardens "Reiman Gardens"), owned by Iowa State University, is a public garden located in Ames.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ames,_Iowa&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Government")]

See also: [List of mayors of Ames, Iowa](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Ames,_Iowa "List of mayors of Ames, Iowa")

From 1979 through 2011, Ames was the location of the [Ames Straw Poll](/wiki/Ames_Straw_Poll "Ames Straw Poll"), which was held every August prior to a presidential election year in which the Republican presidential nomination was undecided (meaning there was no Republican president running for re-election—as in 2011, 2007, 1999, 1995, 1987, and 1979). The poll would gauge support for the various Republican candidates amongst attendees of a [fundraising](/wiki/Fundraising "Fundraising") dinner benefiting the [Iowa Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_of_Iowa "Republican Party of Iowa"). The straw poll was frequently seen by national media and party insiders as a first test of organizational strength in Iowa.[[38]](#cite_note-38) In 2015, the straw poll was to be moved to nearby Boone before the Iowa Republican Party eventually decided to cancel it altogether.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Ames is part of [Iowa House of Representatives District 50](/wiki/Iowa%27s_50th_House_of_Representatives_district "Iowa's 50th House of Representatives district"), currently represented by [Ross Wilburn](/wiki/Ross_Wilburn "Ross Wilburn").[[40]](#cite_note-40) It is part of [Iowa Senate District 25](/wiki/Iowa%27s_25th_Senate_district "Iowa's 25th Senate district"), currently represented by [Herman Quirmbach](/wiki/Herman_Quirmbach "Herman Quirmbach").[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Notable buildings on the university campus include the [Farm House Museum](/wiki/Farm_House_Museum "Farm House Museum"), [Beardshear Hall](/wiki/Beardshear_Hall "Beardshear Hall"), [Morrill Hall](/wiki/Morrill_Hall_(Iowa_State_University) "Morrill Hall (Iowa State University)"), [Memorial Union](/wiki/Memorial_Union_(Iowa_State_University) "Memorial Union (Iowa State University)"), [Catt Hall](/wiki/Catt_Hall "Catt Hall"), Curtiss Hall, [Carver Hall](/wiki/Carver_Hall "Carver Hall"), Parks Library, the [Campanile](/wiki/Campanile_(Iowa_State_University) "Campanile (Iowa State University)"), [Hilton Coliseum](/wiki/Hilton_Coliseum "Hilton Coliseum"), C.Y. Stephens Auditorium, [Fisher Theater](/wiki/Campus_of_Iowa_State_University#Current_buildings "Campus of Iowa State University"), [Jack Trice Stadium](/wiki/Jack_Trice_Stadium "Jack Trice Stadium"), Lied Recreation Center, and numerous residence halls.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [KOEZ](/wiki/KOEZ_(FM) "KOEZ (FM)"), [Adult Contemporary](/wiki/Adult_Contemporary "Adult Contemporary") station licensed to Ames, but operated in Des Moines.
* [KCYZ](/wiki/KCYZ "KCYZ"), [Hot Adult Contemporary](/wiki/Hot_Adult_Contemporary "Hot Adult Contemporary") station owned and operated by Clear Channel in Ames.
* [KASI](/wiki/KASI "KASI"), news/talk station owned and operated by Clear Channel in Ames.
* [KNWM-FM](/wiki/KNWI "KNWI"), [Contemporary Christian](/wiki/Contemporary_Christian_music "Contemporary Christian music") [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid,_Iowa "Madrid, Iowa")/Ames station owned and operated by the University of Northwestern – St. Paul - simulcast with [KNWI-FM](/wiki/KNWI "KNWI") [Osceola](/wiki/Osceola,_Iowa "Osceola, Iowa")/[Des Moines](/wiki/Des_Moines,_Iowa "Des Moines, Iowa")
* [KHOI](/wiki/KHOI_(FM) "KHOI (FM)"), [Community Radio](/wiki/Community_radio "Community radio") station licensed to [Story City](/wiki/Story_City,_Iowa "Story City, Iowa") with studios in Ames. KHOI broadcasts music and local public affairs programs and is affiliated with the [Pacifica Radio](/wiki/Pacifica_Radio "Pacifica Radio") network.

Ames is also served by stations in the Des Moines media market, which includes [Clear Channel's](/wiki/Clear_Channel_Communications "Clear Channel Communications") 50,000-watt talk station [WHO](/wiki/WHO_(AM) "WHO (AM)"), music stations [KAZR](/wiki/KAZR "KAZR"), [KDRB](/wiki/KDRB "KDRB"), [KGGO](/wiki/KGGO "KGGO"), [KKDM](/wiki/KKDM "KKDM"), [KHKI](/wiki/KHKI "KHKI"), [KIOA](/wiki/KIOA "KIOA"), [KJJY](/wiki/KJJY "KJJY"), [KRNT](/wiki/KRNT "KRNT"), [KSPZ](/wiki/KSPZ "KSPZ") and [KSTZ](/wiki/KSTZ "KSTZ"), talk station [KWQW](/wiki/KWQW "KWQW"), and sports stations [KXNO](/wiki/KXNO_(AM) "KXNO (AM)") and [KXNO-FM](/wiki/KXNO-FM "KXNO-FM").

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ames,_Iowa&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Television")]

Ames is served by the Des Moines media market. [WOI-DT](/wiki/WOI-DT "WOI-DT"), the [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") affiliate in central Iowa, was originally owned and operated by Iowa State University until the 1990s. The station is licensed to Ames, with studio's located in [West Des Moines](/wiki/West_Des_Moines "West Des Moines"). Other stations serving Ames include [KCCI](/wiki/KCCI "KCCI"), [KDIN-TV](/wiki/KDIN-TV "KDIN-TV"), [WHO-DT](/wiki/WHO-DT "WHO-DT"), [KCWI-TV](/wiki/KCWI-TV "KCWI-TV"), [KDMI](/wiki/KDMI "KDMI"), [KDSM-TV](/wiki/KDSM-TV "KDSM-TV") and [KFPX-TV](/wiki/KFPX-TV "KFPX-TV").

Channel 12 is owned by the City of Ames, broadcasting city council meetings and local events. The station has received a NATOA Government Programming Award and a Telly Award.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Channel 16 is a public access TV channel.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ames,_Iowa&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Power_plant_in_Ames.jpg/250px-Power_plant_in_Ames.jpg)](/wiki/File:Power_plant_in_Ames.jpg)

City power plant at night blows steam into the air

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ames,_Iowa&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Highways include [U.S. Highways 30](/wiki/U.S._Route_30_in_Iowa "U.S. Route 30 in Iowa"), [69](/wiki/U.S._Route_69_in_Iowa "U.S. Route 69 in Iowa") and [Interstate 35](/wiki/Interstate_35_in_Iowa "Interstate 35 in Iowa").

[Ames Municipal Airport](/wiki/Ames_Municipal_Airport "Ames Municipal Airport") is located 1 mile (1.6 km) southeast of the city.

[CyRide](/wiki/CyRide "CyRide") is a local bus system, and [Jefferson Lines](/wiki/Jefferson_Lines "Jefferson Lines") is an intercity bus.

Ames is home to the headquarters of the [Iowa Department of Transportation](/wiki/Iowa_Department_of_Transportation "Iowa Department of Transportation").[[45]](#cite_note-45)

### Health care

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ames,_Iowa&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Health care")]

Ames is served by [Mary Greeley Medical Center](/wiki/Mary_Greeley_Medical_Center "Mary Greeley Medical Center"), a 220-bed regional hospital. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
